student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Field English Under Pressure -- Managing a Factory Acceptance Test and an On-Site Crisis
lesson.menu_desc: You are on the factory floor in Germany for a witness test on a relay-based interlocking, and halfway through the functional test a fault appears. No script, no time to translate -- you have to read the finding, ask the right questions, document it out loud, and give your project director a clear status report, all in English and all under pressure. Tonight you build the field vocabulary that names what you see (defect, snag, failure mode, degraded mode, witness test, functional test, type approval, ATP, relay, trackside equipment, downtime, pre-commissioning) and you put to work three tenses you already own -- past perfect and past perfect continuous to reconstruct exactly what had happened before the fault, and future perfect to commit to what you will have verified by the witness test. You read a real Factory Acceptance Test field report the way an inspector reads it, you run the fault call with Petra Vogel, the supplier's test lead, and you brief the project director on what was found and what the plan is. This is application, not new grammar: the tenses you learned for reports, now spoken on your feet on a factory floor -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 20 -- Field English Under Pressure
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 20 -- Field English Under Pressure

listenings.text: João, before you walk onto that factory floor for the witness test, let me tell you how I want you to report anything that goes wrong, because on site the language is the difference between a controlled finding and a panic. First, when a fault appears mid-test, do not narrate it in the simple past like a story. Reconstruct the sequence. Say what had already happened before you noticed the problem. If the system had been running in degraded mode for six hours before anyone flagged it, say exactly that, because the past perfect tells the supplier that the fault came first and the symptom came later, and that is the whole of root cause analysis. The engineer who says the relay failed after we had completed the pre-commissioning checklist sounds precise and in control. The one who just says the relay failed sounds like he is guessing. Second, name what you see with the right word. A defect is a defect, a snag is a small one you can clear later, a failure mode is the specific way the thing broke. Do not soften a defect into a problem or inflate a snag into a crisis. The supplier reads your vocabulary as your competence. Third, commit to the future with the future perfect. When the project director asks where you will be by Friday, do not say we will verify the tables, say we will have verified all the interlocking tables before the witness test on Friday. It tells everyone the deadline is real and the work will be finished, not merely started. And last, when you are not sure what someone said in a heavy accent, do not nod and hope. Ask. Can you walk me through this. Is this documented anywhere. Who has authority to sign this off. Repair the communication out loud, because a witness test is no place to guess. Read the fault, name it precisely, reconstruct what had happened, and commit clearly to what you will have done. That is field English under pressure.

Three rules for surviving a factory acceptance test in a language that is not your first, from someone who has stood on a lot of cold factory floors with a clipboard. First, slow the moment down before you speak. When a fault appears, the pressure makes you want to react instantly, and that is when your English collapses into the present tense and vague words. Take one breath and reconstruct the timeline. What had happened before the symptom showed. Had the equipment already passed the functional test when it failed, or had it been failing quietly the whole time. The past perfect is not decoration here, it is the tool that separates the cause from the effect, and on a witness test that distinction decides who is responsible. Second, protect your right to not understand. A supplier's engineer speaking fast, with a heavy accent, over the noise of a workshop, is hard for anyone. The weak move is to nod and pretend. The strong move is to ask, calmly, every time. Can you walk me through this. Is this documented anywhere. We need to put this on hold until we clarify. Asking is not weakness on site, it is exactly what a serious inspector does, and it protects you when the report is written later. Third, close every finding with a plan, not a worry. Do not leave a defect hanging in the air. Say what you will have done and by when. I will have logged the non-conformance today, and we will have re-run the functional test before the witness test on Friday. A defect with a deadline is a managed risk. A defect without one is a crisis. Reconstruct what had happened, ask until you are sure, and close with what you will have done. Do that, and you will run the floor in English as calmly as you run it in Portuguese.

patterns.title: Three tenses you already own -- now spoken under field pressure
cards: Past perfect -- what had happened BEFORE

"By the time we identified the fault, the system HAD been running in degraded mode for six hours." "The supplier HAD NOT completed the pre-commissioning checklist before we arrived on site." Form: had + past participle. On a witness test it does the real work of root cause analysis -- it puts the cause first and the symptom second, so it is clear what happened before the problem showed.
Past perfect continuous -- how LONG it had been going

"The relay HAD BEEN failing intermittently for two days before it dropped out completely." "We HAD BEEN running the functional test for an hour when the alarm came up." Form: had been + -ing. Use it when the point is not just that something happened before, but that it had been going on for a while -- exactly what an inspector needs to reconstruct a failure mode.
Future perfect -- what you WILL HAVE done by the deadline
The commitment that closes a finding

"We WILL HAVE verified all the interlocking tables before the witness test on Friday." "By the end of the shift I WILL HAVE logged the non-conformance and cleared the two snags." Form: will have + past participle. It turns a worry into a managed plan: it tells the project director the work will be finished by a named point, not merely started.

patterns.text: None of these three tenses is new -- you met the past perfect and the past perfect continuous for reports, and the future perfect for commitments. What changes tonight is the CONDITION: you speak them on your feet, on a factory floor, with a fault in front of you and a supplier watching. Under pressure the untrained instinct is to flatten everything into the simple past and present -- "the relay fails, we look, it is broken." The trained one reconstructs the sequence -- "the relay had been failing before the test, so by Friday we will have replaced it." Same facts, opposite level of control. The tense is what makes you sound like an inspector, not a bystander.

reading.rtitle: Factory Acceptance Test — Field Report Extract, Section 3: Finding and Root Cause (adapted, written for João's package)
paras: 3.1 Sequence of events. The witness test on the relay-based interlocking began at 09:00. By the time the inspection team arrived on site, the supplier had not completed the pre-commissioning checklist, and two items had been left open from the previous shift. The functional test ran normally for the first hour. At 10:14 an alarm was raised on the down-line ATP logic. Analysis of the event log showed that the affected relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before — the system had been running in degraded mode for roughly six hours before the alarm made the defect visible.

3.2 Failure mode and impact. The confirmed failure mode was an intermittent contact on a single trackside relay; the trackside equipment around it was unaffected. Because the interlocking had already fallen back to degraded mode as designed, no unsafe command was issued, but the fault caused four hours of test downtime. Two smaller snags — a loose label and a missing cable tag — were noted separately; neither blocks completion and both can be cleared on the punch list. The unit's type approval remains valid, since the defect is an installation fault, not a design fault.

3.3 Corrective action and commitments. The non-conformance was logged on site and escalated to the project manager. The supplier will replace the relay and re-run the affected sequence; by the close of this shift the team will have re-tested the ATP logic, and before the re-scheduled witness test on Friday we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end. Work will not resume on that function until the replacement has passed its own functional test. In short: had the checklist been completed before we arrived, the intermittent relay would have been caught in pre-commissioning rather than under witness — which is exactly the lesson for the next FAT.
reading.source: Motiva Signaling -- FAT field report extract, relay-based interlocking (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Read Section 3 by its tenses and the whole report organizes itself. 3.1 is past perfect and past perfect continuous: it reconstructs what HAD happened and how long the relay HAD BEEN dropping out before the alarm -- the cause before the symptom. 3.2 names the fault precisely -- failure mode, degraded mode, downtime, snag -- so the reader knows exactly how serious it is. 3.3 is future perfect: what the team WILL HAVE done by the shift and by Friday, which turns the finding into a managed plan. Same fault, told in three tenses: what had happened, what it is, and what will have been done about it. That is a field report that sounds in control.

gist.prompt: What is Section 3 of the field report mainly doing?
choices: Cancelling the factory acceptance test and rejecting the interlocking as unsafe.

Reconstructing what had happened before the fault, naming the failure mode precisely, and committing to what will have been done by the re-scheduled witness test -- a managed non-conformance, not a crisis.
Listing the commercial price and the delivery schedule for the trackside equipment.
Recording that the unit lost its type approval because of a design fault.

items: By the time the inspection team arrived, the supplier had already completed the pre-commissioning checklist.
f

The opposite: "the supplier had not completed the pre-commissioning checklist, and two items had been left open from the previous shift." The past perfect makes the missing prep the cause that came first.
The relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before, so the system had been running in degraded mode for about six hours before the alarm.
t
"...the affected relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before, the system had been running in degraded mode for roughly six hours before the alarm made the defect visible."
Because the interlocking fell back to degraded mode as designed, an unsafe command was issued to the trackside equipment.
f
"Because the interlocking had already fallen back to degraded mode as designed, no unsafe command was issued." Degraded mode is the safe fallback -- that is the point.
The unit lost its type approval because the fault was a design fault.
f
"The unit's type approval remains valid, since the defect is an installation fault, not a design fault." The two smaller items are snags for the punch list.
Before the re-scheduled witness test on Friday, the team will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end.
t
"...before the re-scheduled witness test on Friday we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end." The future perfect commits to work finished by a named point.

items.situation: A fault appears mid-test. Petra asks what happened. Reconstruct the sequence with the past perfect, don't just narrate it in the simple past.
tips: By the time we identified the fault, the system had been running in degraded mode for about six hours -- the relay had been dropping out since the night before.
The alarm came up at 10:14, but the relay had already been failing intermittently before the test even started -- that's the root cause, not the symptom.

items.situation: The project director asks where you will be by Friday. Commit with the future perfect, not a vague promise.
tips: Before the witness test on Friday we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end, and by the close of this shift we will have re-tested the ATP logic.
By Friday the relay will have been replaced and the affected sequence re-run -- I'll have logged the non-conformance today.

items.situation: Petra calls the two minor items a crisis. Name them precisely -- are they defects or snags?
tips: They're snags, not defects that block completion -- a loose label and a missing cable tag. Both go on the punch list and can be cleared later.
Let's keep the words precise: the intermittent relay is the defect and the failure mode; the label and the tag are just snags.

items.situation: You didn't catch what the supplier's engineer said over the workshop noise. Don't nod and hope -- repair the communication.
tips: Sorry, can you walk me through this again? I want to make sure I've got the failure mode right before I document it.
I didn't quite catch that -- is this documented anywhere? We need to put this on hold until we clarify.

items.situation: Petra wants her team to keep working on the affected function while the relay is replaced. State the limit.
tips: Work will not resume on that function until the replacement relay has passed its own functional test -- I have to flag this before we proceed.
Let's hold that function: I'd like to escalate to the project manager first and clear it before anyone signs it off.

items.situation: The director asks whether the fault affects the unit's certification. Answer precisely, using type approval and failure mode.
tips: No -- the type approval stays valid. The failure mode is an intermittent contact on one trackside relay, an installation fault, not a design fault.
The design certification holds: it's an installation-level defect on a single relay, so the type approval isn't affected.

bank.label: Reconstructing the sequence (past perfect)
items: By the time we identified the fault, the system had been running in degraded mode for about six hours.
The supplier had not completed the pre-commissioning checklist before we arrived on site.
The relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before -- that's the root cause, not the symptom.

bank.label: Committing to the plan (future perfect)
items: Before the witness test on Friday, we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end.
By the close of this shift, the team will have re-tested the ATP logic and cleared the two snags.
I'll have logged the non-conformance today, and the relay will have been replaced by tomorrow.

bank.label: Naming what you see precisely
items: That's the defect and the failure mode -- an intermittent contact on a single trackside relay.
Those two are snags, not blockers: a loose label and a missing cable tag for the punch list.
The interlocking fell back to degraded mode as designed, so no unsafe command was issued.

bank.label: Survival phrases for the floor
items: Can you walk me through this? Is this documented anywhere?
We need to put this on hold until we clarify -- I'd like to flag this before we proceed.
Who has authority to sign this off? I'll escalate it to the project manager.
